answers.intake_q1: When did your elbow pain start?
answers.intake_q2: Can you describe where exactly on your elbow the pain is located?
answers.intake_q3: What activities or injuries, if any, preceded the onset of your elbow pain?
answers.intake_a3: I was playing tennis and it started hurting afterwards
answers.intake_q4: Any swelling, redness, or warmth around the elbow?
answers.intake_q5: Any numbness, tingling, or weakness in your hand or arm?
answers.intake_q6: Have you tried any treatments or medications for your elbow pain, and if so, what were they and did they help?
answers.intake_a6: icing the day it started and it helped a little
answers.intake_q7: Any difficulty gripping or lifting objects with your affected arm?
answers.intake_q8: Any fever, chills, or other systemic symptoms?
answers.intake_q9: Have you noticed any clicking, locking, or instability in your elbow when moving it?
answers.intake_q10: Have you had any prior elbow injuries or conditions on this arm?

note.soap:
S: 
The patient is a 45-year-old female presenting with a 3-week history of pain localized to the outside of her elbow. The pain began after playing tennis and is associated with discomfort when lifting objects. She reports no swelling, redness, warmth, numbness, tingling, weakness, fever, chills, or systemic symptoms. She has tried icing on the day the pain started, which provided some relief. There is no history of prior elbow injuries or conditions, and no clicking, locking, or instability is noted. Patient is not pregnant.

O: 
Phone evaluation only; no physical exam or vitals available.

A: 
The clinical presentation is consistent with lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow), characterized by pain over the lateral elbow following repetitive wrist extension activities such as tennis. This is the working diagnosis given the history and symptom localization.

Differential: 
1. Lateral epicondylitis (tennis elbow) 
2. Radial tunnel syndrome 
3. Osteoarthritis of the elbow 
4. Referred pain from cervical radiculopathy 

P: 
- First-line medication: Nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen or naproxen for pain relief and inflammation reduction. 
- Alternatives: Acetaminophen if NSAIDs are contraindicated or not tolerated. 
- Medication safety considerations: Use NSAIDs with caution in patients with gastrointestinal, renal, or cardiovascular risk factors; ensure dosing does not exceed recommended limits. 
- Return precautions: Advise the patient to seek care if symptoms worsen, if there is new onset of numbness, tingling, weakness, swelling, redness, or fever, or if pain does not improve with conservative management within 4 to 6 weeks. 
- Testing: Not indicated at this time; consider imaging or referral if symptoms persist or worsen despite treatment.

evidence.title: Tennis elbow - NHS
evidence.snippet: rvices without needing a referral from a GP. Get NHS help for back or joint problems Treatments for tennis elbow Tennis elbow usually gets better after resting your arm for a few weeks, but it can sometimes last longer. A GP may give you anti-inflammatory tablets. They may also refer you for physiotherapy if your symptoms have not improved after trying treat…

evidence.snippet: bone, cartilage, ligaments and fluid. Muscles and tendons help the elbow joint move. When any of these structures is hurt or diseased, you have elbow problems. Many things can make your elbow hurt. A common cause is tendinitis , an inflammation or injury to the tendons that attach muscle to bone. Tendinitis of the elbow is a sports injury , often from playin…

evidence.title: Septic arthritisÂ | Infectious Arthritis | MedlinePlus
evidence.snippet: s Summary Most kinds of arthritis cause pain and swelling in your joints. Joints are places where two bones meet, such as your elbow or knee. Infectious arthritis is an infection in the joint. The infection comes from a bacterial, viral, or fungal infection that spreads from another part of the body. evidence.snippet: joints. RA affects people differently. You might have the disease for only a short time, or your symptoms might come and go. The severe form can be chronic (long-lasting) and may last a lifetime. Symptoms of rheumatoid arthritis may include: Joint pain or stiffness when moving.
